parts.content: Explain how the type of fusion reaction changes as a star ages and its core temperature increases, including the transition from hydrogen to helium fusion.
parts.markscheme:
### Main Idea
As a star ages and its core temperature increases, the type of fusion reaction changes from hydrogen fusion to helium fusion.

- At lower core temperatures, hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium nuclei via the proton-proton chain reaction:
$$4\,{}^1_1\text{H} \rightarrow {}^4_2\text{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + \text{energy}$$
This is the main fusion reaction in stars like the Sun. <marks>A1</marks>

- As the star ages and the core temperature rises above about 100 million K, the helium nuclei produced by hydrogen fusion can fuse further via the triple-alpha process:
$$3\,{}^4_2\text{He} \rightarrow {}^{12}_6\text{C} + \text{energy}$$
This helium fusion reaction becomes the dominant process. <marks>A1</marks>

- Explanation that the transition is driven by the core contracting and heating up as hydrogen fuel is depleted. <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks if the transition from hydrogen to helium fusion is not clearly explained.</note>

<examTip>Clearly distinguish between the two fusion processes and the conditions under which each occurs.</examTip>

steps.title: Hydrogen fusion in main sequence
steps.content:
For most of its life, a star is in the **main sequence** stage. During this time, the core temperature is high enough (millions of Kelvin) to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between protons. Hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium nuclei, primarily through the **proton-proton chain reaction**:

$$4\,{}^1_1\text{H} \rightarrow {}^4_2\text{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + \text{energy}$$

This process provides the outward radiation pressure that balances the inward pull of gravity, keeping the star stable. This is covered in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**.

steps.title: Core contraction and heating
steps.content:
As the star ages, the hydrogen fuel in the core is eventually depleted. With no more hydrogen fusion to provide outward pressure, the core begins to contract under its own gravity. 

This contraction converts gravitational potential energy into thermal energy, causing the core temperature to rise sharply. This is the critical mechanism behind the **transition from hydrogen to helium fusion**.

highlights.text: transition from hydrogen to helium fusion

steps.title: The triple-alpha process
steps.content:
Once the core temperature reaches approximately $10^8 \text{ K}$ (100 million K), the helium nuclei produced during the main sequence stage have enough kinetic energy to fuse. 

This occurs via the **triple-alpha process**, where three helium nuclei (alpha particles) fuse to form a carbon nucleus:

$$3\,{}^4_2\text{He} \rightarrow {}^{12}_6\text{C} + \text{energy}$$

This new source of energy causes the outer layers of the star to expand, marking its evolution into a Red Giant.

parts.content: Describe the role of helium fusion in the formation of heavier elements during a star's later stages.
parts.markscheme:
### Helium fusion

- Helium fusion occurs when the core temperature reaches around 100 million K. <marks>M1</marks>
- This allows helium nuclei to fuse into carbon via the triple-alpha process:
$$
\begin{align*}
{}^4_2\text{He} + {}^4_2\text{He} &\rightarrow {}^8_4\text{Be} \\
{}^8_4\text{Be} + {}^4_2\text{He} &\rightarrow {}^{12}_6\text{C} + \gamma
\end{align*}
$$
<marks>M1</marks>

<note>Award M1 for a relevant statement about helium fusion or the triple-alpha process. Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

steps.title: Transition to helium fusion
steps.content: In the later stages of a star's life, once hydrogen in the core is exhausted, the core contracts under its own gravity. This contraction increases the pressure and kinetic energy of the particles, leading to a dramatic rise in the core's physical temperature.

steps.title: Core temperature requirements
steps.content: Helium nuclei (alpha particles) have a higher positive charge than hydrogen nuclei, meaning they experience a stronger electrostatic repulsion. To overcome this repulsion and allow the nuclei to get close enough for the strong nuclear force to take over, the physical environment must be extremely hot. Helium fusion only begins when the core temperature reaches approximately $100$ million K ($10^8$ K).

highlights.text: Helium fusion occurs when the core temperature reaches around 100 million K.

steps.title: The Triple-Alpha Process
steps.content:
Under these extreme thermal conditions, the star undergoes the **triple-alpha process**. This is a two-stage fusion reaction where three helium nuclei eventually form a carbon nucleus. 

$$\begin{aligned} {}^4_2\text{He} + {}^4_2\text{He} &\rightarrow {}^8_4\text{Be} \\ {}^8_4\text{Be} + {}^4_2\text{He} &\rightarrow {}^{12}_6\text{C} + \gamma \end{aligned}$$

This process is the primary way that carbon, a vital building block for heavier elements, is created in the universe.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention:
1. The specific temperature condition ($100$ million K) required for the environment. <marks>M1</marks>
2. That this fusion leads to the formation of carbon (often referencing the triple-alpha process). <marks>M1</marks>

steps.title: Identify required core conditions
steps.content: For helium fusion to occur, the star's core must reach a much higher temperature than that required for hydrogen fusion. Specifically, the core temperature must reach approximately $10^8\text{ K}$ (100 million Kelvin) to provide the helium nuclei with enough kinetic energy to overcome their mutual electrostatic repulsion.

highlights.text: core temperature reaches around 100 million K

steps.title: Formation of beryllium-8
steps.content:
The first step in the **triple-alpha process** involves the fusion of two helium-4 nuclei (also known as alpha particles). These two nuclei combine to form a beryllium-8 nucleus:

$${}^4_2\text{He} + {}^4_2\text{He} \rightarrow {}^8_4\text{Be}$$

steps.title: Synthesis of carbon-12
steps.content:
Beryllium-8 is highly unstable and has a very short half-life. However, in the dense, hot core of a late-stage star, a third helium-4 nucleus can fuse with the beryllium-8 before it decays. This completes the triple-alpha process, resulting in a stable carbon-12 nucleus and the release of energy in the form of a gamma photon:

$${}^8_4\text{Be} + {}^4_2\text{He} \rightarrow {}^{12}_6\text{C} + \gamma$$

steps.title: Relate to stellar evolution
steps.content: This mechanism allows stars to move beyond hydrogen burning and begin synthesizing heavier elements. The carbon produced in this pathway serves as the building block for even heavier elements in more massive stars. This topic is part of **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**, which covers how nuclear reactions drive stellar life cycles.

parts.content: Discuss how the mass of a star determines its final stage, including possibilities like white dwarfs or supernovae, and the implications for the universe.
parts.markscheme:
### Mass determines final stage of star
- Stars with mass less than about 8 solar masses end as white dwarfs. <marks>M1</marks>
  - When star runs out of fuel for fusion
  - Gravitational forces balanced by degenerate electron pressure
  - Prevents further collapse into neutron star/black hole

- Stars with mass greater than about 8 solar masses end in supernova. <marks>M1</marks>
  - Iron core builds up, fusion no longer releases energy
  - Core collapses under gravity, rebound causes explosive ejection of outer layers

### Implications for universe
- White dwarfs: source of planetary nebulae, enriching interstellar medium.
- Supernovae:
    - Disperse heavy elements from star's interior into space
    - Drive formation of new stars/star systems from ejected material
    - Neutron stars/pulsars remain after core collapse
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: The Role of Mass
steps.content:
In stellar evolution, the initial mass of a star is the primary factor that determines its lifecycle and ultimate fate. IB Physics categorizes stars into two main groups based on a threshold of approximately 8 solar masses ($8 M_{\odot}$).

This threshold determines whether a star will end its life relatively peacefully or in a violent explosion.

steps.title: Stars below 8 Solar Masses
steps.content:
Stars with an initial mass less than about $8 M_{\odot}$ (like our Sun) follow this path:

*   **Evolution & End-state:** Once hydrogen and helium fusion ceases in the core, the star sheds its outer layers as a **planetary nebula**. The remaining core collapses under gravity until it is balanced by **electron degeneracy pressure**. This prevents further collapse, resulting in a stable **white dwarf**.
*   **Cosmic Impact:** These stars enrich the interstellar medium with elements like carbon and oxygen through their planetary nebulae, providing the raw materials for future planetary systems.

steps.title: Stars above 8 Solar Masses
steps.content:
Stars with an initial mass greater than $8 M_{\odot}$ undergo a much more dramatic transformation:

*   **Evolution & End-state:** These stars can fuse heavier elements until an **iron core** builds up. Because iron fusion requires energy rather than releasing it, the core suddenly loses its outward pressure and collapses under gravity. The outer layers then rebound off the dense core, causing a massive **supernova** explosion. Depending on the remaining mass, the core becomes a **neutron star** or a **black hole**.
*   **Cosmic Impact:** Supernovae are essential for the universe because they create and disperse heavy elements (heavier than iron) into space. The shockwaves from these explosions can also trigger the gravitational collapse of nearby gas clouds, leading to the **formation of new stars**.

steps.title: Summary of Cosmic Implications
steps.content:
The distribution of mass in the stellar population ensures a continuous cycle of matter:

1.  **Low-mass stars** provide steady enrichment of the interstellar medium.
2.  **High-mass stars** provide the heavy elements necessary for rocky planets and life, while driving the birth of new generations of stars.

To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention the **8 solar mass threshold**, the specific **end-states** (white dwarf vs supernova), and how these processes **recycle material** back into the universe.

steps.title: White Dwarfs: Low-Mass Stars
steps.content:
The final stage of a star is determined by its initial mass. Stars with a mass less than approximately 8 solar masses ($M < 8 M_{\odot}$) end their lifecycle as **white dwarfs**.

Once these stars exhaust their nuclear fuel, fusion stops. The star sheds its outer layers as a planetary nebula, leaving behind a dense core. This core is prevented from collapsing further by **electron degeneracy pressure**, which provides the outward pressure necessary to balance the inward force of gravity.

steps.title: Supernovae: High-Mass Stars
steps.content:
Stars with a mass greater than approximately 8 solar masses ($M > 8 M_{\odot}$) reach a more violent end known as a **Type II Supernova**.

In these massive stars, fusion continues until an iron core is formed. Because iron fusion does not release energy, the core can no longer support itself against gravity and suddenly collapses. This collapse results in a massive explosion that ejections the star's outer layers into space, leaving behind a neutron star or a black hole.

steps.title: Implications for the Universe
steps.content:
The final stages of stars are vital for the chemical evolution of the universe:

*   **Enrichment:** Both planetary nebulae (from white dwarfs) and supernovae disperse elements created during the star's life into the interstellar medium.
*   **Heavy Elements:** Supernovae are particularly significant as they are responsible for creating and distributing elements heavier than iron.
*   **Star Formation:** The material ejected and the shockwaves from supernovae drive the formation of new generations of stars and planetary systems, recycled from the remains of older stars.

steps.title: Mass and Equilibrium
steps.content:
In stellar physics, the life and death of a star are defined by a constant battle between the inward pull of gravity and the outward pressure from fusion. The star's initial mass determines which force eventually wins and what physical mechanism halts the final collapse.

This topic is covered in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**. If you need a refresher on how stars maintain their size, you might also want to review **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

steps.title: Mechanism for Low-Mass Stars
steps.content:
For stars with a mass less than approximately **8 solar masses**, the final stage is a **white dwarf**. 

When these stars run out of nuclear fuel, fusion stops and gravity begins to compress the core. However, the collapse is halted by **electron degeneracy pressure**. This is a quantum mechanical effect where electrons are packed so tightly that they resist further compression. This pressure balances the gravitational force, leaving behind a dense, cooling core.

highlights.text: mass less than about 8 solar masses

steps.title: Mechanism for High-Mass Stars
steps.content:
Stars with a mass greater than **8 solar masses** undergo a much more violent end. These stars can fuse heavier elements until an **iron core** forms. 

Since fusing iron requires more energy than it releases (based on $E=mc^{2}$), the outward pressure suddenly vanishes. The core collapses under its own immense gravity in a fraction of a second. This collapse and the subsequent "rebound" create a shockwave that results in a **supernova**—a massive explosive ejection of the star's outer layers.

steps.title: Implications for the Universe
steps.content:
These final stages are essential for the evolution of the universe. 

1. **Enrichment:** Supernovae disperse heavy elements (like iron, gold, and uranium) into space. These elements are only created during the star's life or the explosion itself.
2. **Recycling:** The ejected material forms interstellar clouds that eventually collapse to form new stars and planetary systems. 
3. **Remnants:** The collapsed cores that remain after a supernova may become **neutron stars** or **black holes**, depending on the remaining mass.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention:
- **Low mass (< 8 $M_{\odot}$):** Ends as a white dwarf, supported by electron degeneracy pressure.
- **High mass (> 8 $M_{\odot}$):** Ends in a supernova due to iron core collapse and rebound.
- **Implications:** The distribution of heavy elements and the formation of new star systems.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content: The question asks for the relationship between mass and energy. This concept is fundamental to understanding how stars produce energy through fusion, which is covered in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**.

highlights.text: State the equation that relates mass and energy.

steps.title: Locate the formula
steps.content:
In your IB Physics Data Booklet, you can find the mass-energy equivalence formula in the **Nuclear** section. This equation shows that energy ($E$) is equal to the mass ($m$) multiplied by the speed of light squared ($c^2$).

$$E = mc^2$$

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
To earn the mark, simply state the equation as it appears in the booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

parts.content: State what happens to mass during fusion.
parts.markscheme:
Mass decreases. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the prompt
steps.content: The question provides a very helpful clue: it states that fusion in stars "converts mass to energy." To answer this part, we just need to look at what that conversion implies for the total amount of mass.

highlights.text: Fusion in stars converts mass to energy.

steps.title: Apply logical inference
steps.content:
If mass is being **converted** into energy, it means some of the original material is no longer present as mass because it has been transformed into a different form. 

Logically, if you take a portion of mass and turn it into energy, the remaining mass must be less than what you started with.

steps.title: Connect to mass-energy equivalence
steps.content:
This relationship is defined by Einstein's mass-energy equivalence formula, which you can find in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

In this equation, $E$ represents the energy released and $m$ represents the "mass defect" (the mass that was lost). Because stars release a massive amount of energy through fusion, we know a corresponding amount of mass must have been lost.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: Based on the prompt's statement that mass is converted to energy, we can conclude that the total mass **decreases** during the fusion process.

steps.title: Understand nuclear fusion
steps.content: Nuclear fusion occurs when two light nuclei join together to form a heavier nucleus. This process is what powers stars and involves the release of a massive amount of energy.

highlights.text: Fusion in stars converts mass to energy.

steps.title: Apply mass-energy equivalence
steps.content:
The fundamental principle behind this energy release is mass-energy equivalence. According to the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

This equation shows that energy ($E$) and mass ($m$) are proportional. For energy to be released (produced) during fusion, there must be a corresponding reduction in the total rest mass of the particles involved.

steps.title: Define the mass defect
steps.content:
In a fusion reaction, the mass of the resulting heavier nucleus is always less than the sum of the masses of the original light nuclei. This difference is known as the **mass defect**. 

Because some of the initial mass is converted into the kinetic energy of the products or gamma-ray photons, the total mass of the system **decreases**.

parts.content: State one reason why fusion produces energy.
parts.markscheme:
Mass defect is converted into energy. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand the fusion process
steps.content: In nuclear fusion, light nuclei join together to form a heavier, more stable nucleus. To understand why this process releases energy, we need to look at the total mass before and after the reaction.

steps.title: Identify the mass defect
steps.content: When fusion occurs, the total mass of the resulting products is slightly **less** than the total mass of the original reactants. This difference in mass is known as the **mass defect**.

steps.title: Relate mass to energy
steps.content:
According to the principle of mass-energy equivalence found in your data booklet, mass can be converted into energy. The relationship is given by the formula:

$$E = mc^2$$

Because there is a decrease in mass during fusion, that "missing" mass is released as a significant amount of energy.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The reason fusion produces energy is that the **mass defect** (the lost mass) is **converted into energy**.

steps.title: Analyze the fusion process
steps.content: In nuclear fusion, two light nuclei (the reactants) join together to form a heavier nucleus (the product). This process is covered in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**. To understand why this releases energy, we look at how tightly the nucleons (protons and neutrons) are bound together in the nucleus.

highlights.text: State one reason why fusion produces energy.

steps.title: Compare binding energy
steps.content:
For elements lighter than iron, the product nucleus formed by fusion has a **higher binding energy per nucleon** than the original reactant nuclei. 

Binding energy is essentially the energy released when a nucleus is formed from its individual parts. If the product has a higher binding energy, it means the nucleons are more tightly bound and the system has moved to a more stable, lower-energy state.

steps.title: Relate stability to energy
steps.content:
Because the product is more stable than the reactants, the difference in the total binding energy is released into the surroundings. 

According to the mass-energy equivalence formula in the data booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

This increase in binding energy corresponds to a 'mass defect' where the final mass is slightly less than the initial mass, with that 'missing' mass being converted into the energy produced by the star.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content: The reason fusion produces energy is that the **binding energy per nucleon of the product is higher** than that of the reactants, leading to a release of energy as the system becomes more stable.

parts.content: Identify the fundamental force that must be overcome for fusion to occur.
parts.markscheme:
Electrostatic / Coulomb repulsion. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the fusion process
steps.content: Nuclear fusion is the process where two light atomic nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus. This process occurs in the cores of stars, but for these nuclei to merge, they must be brought extremely close together. This topic is covered in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**.

highlights.text: Fusion in stars converts mass to energy.

steps.title: Identify nuclear charges
steps.content: Every atomic nucleus contains protons, which have a positive charge. Because like charges repel each other, two nuclei will naturally push away from one another as they approach. This is a fundamental concept from **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Determine the repulsive force
steps.content:
The force responsible for this push-back is the **electrostatic force** (also known as the **Coulomb force**). According to Coulomb’s Law, the force between two charges is given by:

$$F=k\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r^{2}}$$

Since both nuclei are positively charged ($q_1$ and $q_2$ are positive), the force $F$ is repulsive. For fusion to happen, the nuclei must have enough kinetic energy (from high temperatures) to overcome this repulsion and get close enough for the strong nuclear force to take over.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must identify the force that resists the nuclei coming together.

**Answer:** Electrostatic force (or Coulomb repulsion).

parts.content: Sketch a graph of binding energy per nucleon vs nucleon number for $A = 1$ to $A = 60$.
parts.markscheme:
![Binding energy per nucleon graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/db92447a-f4f8-4196-853e-8a4e44abddbd.png)

*   Graph rises steeply and levels off around iron.
    [1]
*   Axes labeled: BE/A (or Binding Energy per Nucleon) on $y$-axis, nucleon number (or $A$) on $x$-axis.
    [1]

steps.title: Label the axes
steps.content:
The first step in any graph sketch is to label your axes correctly. 

- The $y$-axis represents the **binding energy per nucleon** (often written as $BE/A$), typically measured in MeV.
- The $x$-axis represents the **nucleon number** (mass number, $A$).

This is a fundamental requirement for earning the first mark.

steps.title: Sketch the steep rise
steps.content:
Start your curve at the origin $(0,0)$. For a single proton ($A=1$), there is no binding energy because there are no other nucleons to bind with. 

From there, draw a **steep gradient** rising quickly as $A$ increases. This represents how small nuclei become much more stable as they gain nucleons through the process of fusion.

steps.title: Plateau at the peak
steps.content:
The curve does not rise forever; it eventually 'levels off' or plateaus. 

The peak of this curve occurs at **Iron-56** ($^{56}\text{Fe}$), which is the most stable nucleus. Since the question asks for the graph up to $A=60$, you should show the curve reaching its maximum height and beginning to flatten out around $A \approx 56$. 

$$A \approx 56 \rightarrow \text{Peak stability}$$

steps.title: Final check for marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get both marks, verify:
1. Your axes are clearly labeled with units ($BE/A$ in MeV and $A$).
2. The shape shows a sharp initial increase followed by a clear leveling off at the top near $A = 60$.

This characteristic shape is central to **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, as it explains why fusion releases energy for light elements and fission releases energy for heavy elements.

steps.title: Define the axes
steps.content:
To start our sketch, we need to label the axes correctly. The question asks for a graph of binding energy per nucleon versus nucleon number.

*   **$y$-axis**: Binding Energy per Nucleon ($	ext{BE}/A$). This is usually measured in $\text{MeV}$.
*   **$x$-axis**: Nucleon Number ($A$).

Identifying the correct labels is worth **1 mark**.

highlights.text: binding energy per nucleon vs nucleon number

steps.title: Identify the starting point
steps.content:
We use the **Stability Principle**: a nucleus is stable because energy is required to pull it apart. 

At $A = 1$ (a single proton or neutron), there are no other nucleons to bind with. Therefore, the binding energy must start at zero. 

$$(\text{BE}/A) = 0 \text{ at } A = 1$$

steps.title: Deduce the fusion trend
steps.content:
For light nuclei ($A < 60$), the nucleons are not yet at their most stable configuration. When small nuclei join together (fusion), they form a larger, more tightly bound nucleus. 

According to the stability principle, this means the **binding energy per nucleon must increase** as $A$ increases because the system is moving toward a more stable state. This results in a steep upward curve from $A = 1$.

highlights.text: Energy is released in fusion due to the change in binding energy per nucleon.

steps.title: Locate the maximum stability
steps.content:
Stability reaches its peak at the "iron group" (specifically $^{56}\text{Fe}$). At this point, the nucleons are packed as efficiently as possible.

Since the question asks us to sketch up to $A = 60$, your curve should:
1.  Rise steeply from the origin.
2.  Begin to level off (flatten out) as it approaches $A = 56$ to $60$.

Achieving this characteristic shape is worth the **second mark**.

steps.title: Final Sketch Summary
steps.content:
Combining these deductions, your sketch should look like a curve that starts at $(1,0)$, goes up sharply, and curves over to become almost horizontal by the time it reaches $A=60$.

Remember, in the IB exam, you don't need to plot every point perfectly, but the **steep rise** and the **leveling off near iron** are critical features to show.

parts.content: Explain how the graph supports energy release by fusion.
parts.markscheme:
*   Fusion combines small nuclei (low $A$), moving to the right on the graph.
    [1]
*   Binding energy per nucleon increases, which means energy is released.
    [1]

steps.title: Locate light nuclei
steps.content:
To understand fusion, we first look at the left side of the binding energy per nucleon graph. This region represents nuclei with a low mass number $A$ (light nuclei), such as Hydrogen and Helium. Notice that in this specific area, the curve has a very steep positive gradient.

This concept is part of **Topic E.3: Stability and binding energy**, which explores how the configuration of a nucleus determines its energy state.

steps.title: Analyze movement during fusion
steps.content:
Nuclear fusion is the process of joining two light nuclei to form a single, larger nucleus. 

On the graph, because the new nucleus has a larger mass number than the original individual nuclei, this process corresponds to moving to the right along the horizontal axis ($A$).

highlights.text: Fusion combines small nuclei (low A), moving to the right on the graph.

steps.title: Relate gradient to energy
steps.content:
Because the graph is sloping upwards (positive gradient) for light nuclei, moving to the right leads to a higher point on the vertical axis. 

This tells us that the product of the fusion reaction has a higher **binding energy per nucleon** than the starting reactants. When nucleons become more tightly bound, the system reaches a lower potential energy state, and the "excess" energy is released to the surroundings.

From the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet, we know that mass and energy are related by:

$$E=mc^{2}$$

highlights.text: Binding energy per nucleon increases, which means energy is released.

steps.title: Identify fusion on the graph
steps.content:
To understand how the graph supports fusion, we first need to look at where fusion happens. Fusion is the process where two small, light nuclei (with a low nucleon number $A$) join together to form a larger, more stable nucleus.

On the graph of binding energy per nucleon versus nucleon number, this corresponds to **moving from left to right** along the x-axis in the region where $A$ is small.

highlights.text: Fusion combines small nuclei (low A), moving to the right on the graph.

steps.title: Observe binding energy change
steps.content:
As we move to the right from very light nuclei, the curve slopes upward. This shows that the **binding energy per nucleon increases** for the resulting larger nucleus compared to the initial smaller nuclei.

This is a key indicator of increased stability in the nuclear structure.

steps.title: Analyze total binding energy
steps.content:
The total binding energy of a nucleus is calculated by multiplying the binding energy per nucleon by the total number of nucleons ($A$):

$$\text{Total Binding Energy} = \left( \frac{BE}{A} \right) \times A$$

In a fusion reaction, the total number of nucleons $A$ remains constant. Since the binding energy *per nucleon* has increased, the **total binding energy of the system increases** after fusion has occurred.

steps.title: Connect to energy release
steps.content:
Binding energy is the energy released when a nucleus is formed from its constituent nucleons. 

Because the product nucleus has a higher total binding energy than the original reactants, the system has effectively "given up" more energy to become more tightly bound. This difference in binding energy is the **energy released** to the surroundings during the fusion process.

parts.content: State the approximate binding energy per nucleon for helium.
parts.markscheme:
About $7 \text{ MeV}$.
[1]

steps.title: Recall the Binding Energy Curve
steps.content:
In nuclear physics, we often use a graph of **binding energy per nucleon** versus **mass number ($A$)**. This curve shows how tightly bound the nucleons (protons and neutrons) are in different nuclei. 

This concept is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, where you learn that nuclei with higher binding energy per nucleon are generally more stable.

steps.title: Identify the Helium Peak
steps.content: While the curve generally rises for light nuclei and peaks near Iron-56, there is a very prominent "spike" or local maximum at the beginning of the graph. This peak corresponds to Helium-4 ($^4\text{He}$), also known as an alpha particle, which is exceptionally stable for its small size.

highlights.text: State the approximate binding energy per nucleon for helium.

steps.title: State the Approximate Value
steps.content:
From the standard binding energy curve taught in the IB syllabus, you should recall that the binding energy per nucleon for Helium-4 is approximately:

$$BE/\text{nucleon} \approx 7 \text{ MeV}$$

(More precisely, it is about $7.07 \text{ MeV}$, but the IB markscheme accepts approximately $7 \text{ MeV}$ for this state-style question.)

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To find the distance to the star, we first look at the information provided in the question. We are given the parallax angle ($p$), which is a measure of how much the star appears to move against distant background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.

highlights.text: parallax angle of a star is measured to be 0.02 arcseconds

steps.title: Use the parallax formula
steps.content:
In IB Physics, specifically in **Topic E.5: Fusion and stars**, we learn that the distance ($d$) to a star in parsecs ($pc$) is the reciprocal of its parallax angle ($p$) in arcseconds. This is a fundamental definition used to measure stellar distances.

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{1}{p} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the distance
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given parallax angle $p = 0.02$ arcseconds into our formula to find the distance:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{1}{0.02} \\ d &= 50 \text{ pc} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 1 / 0.02 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 0.02 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal of the parallax angle to find the distance in parsecs.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The distance to the star is **50 pc**.

In an IB exam, you would earn:
*   **1 mark** for correctly identifying and stating the formula $d = \frac{1}{p}$.
*   **1 mark** for the correct final answer with the appropriate unit (parsecs).

parts.content: Using the Stefan-Boltzmann law, $L=4 \pi R^2 \sigma T^4$, calculate the radius of the star. Use $\sigma=$ $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{~K}^4$ and assume $L_{\odot}=3.83 \times 10^{26} \mathrm{~W}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Express luminosity in terms of radius and rearrange for $R$: $L = 4 \pi R^2 \sigma T^4 \Rightarrow R = \sqrt{\frac{L}{4 \pi \sigma T^4}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate luminosity: $L = 100 \times 3.83 \times 10^{26} = 3.83 \times 10^{28} \text{ W}$

- Substitute values: $R = \sqrt{\frac{3.83 \times 10^{28}}{4 \pi \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (6000)^4}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final radius: $R \approx 6.4 \times 10^9 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain how the balance between fusion energy and gravitational forces affects the star's stability over time.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify outward force: Nuclear fusion in star's core releases energy creating outward thermal pressure <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify inward force: Gravitational forces act inward, compressing the star's mass <marks>A1</marks>

- State condition for stability: When these forces are in balance (hydrostatic equilibrium), the star remains stable <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award marks for clear understanding of the opposing forces and their role in stability, even if different wording is used</note>

steps.title: Identify the inward force
steps.content:
The first major player in a star's stability is gravity. Because a star has such an immense mass, it generates a powerful gravitational field. This force acts inward, pulling all the star's material toward the core and attempting to compress its mass. 

We can think of this using the law of gravitation from the data booklet:

$$F=G\frac{m_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}$$

steps.title: Identify the outward force
steps.content: Opposing gravity is the energy produced in the core. Through the process of nuclear fusion, the star releases a massive amount of energy. This energy creates an **outward thermal pressure** (and radiation pressure) as the high-temperature particles push outward against the weight of the star's outer layers.

steps.title: Define hydrostatic equilibrium
steps.content:
The star remains stable over time when these two opposing forces are exactly equal. This state of balance is called **hydrostatic equilibrium**. 

$$\text{Inward Gravitational Force} = \text{Outward Pressure Force}$$

If fusion were to slow down, gravity would dominate and the star would contract. If fusion increased, the star would expand. Thus, the continuous balance between these forces determines the star's size and stability during its main-sequence lifetime.

highlights.text: balance between fusion energy and gravitational forces

steps.title: Identify inward gravitational force
steps.content: A star is a massive object, and due to its mass, it experiences an immense inward **gravitational force**. This force acts on all the particles within the star, constantly trying to pull the matter toward the center and cause the star to collapse.

highlights.text: Gravitational forces act inward, compressing the star's mass

steps.title: Identify outward fusion pressure
steps.content: To counter gravity, stars generate an outward force through **nuclear fusion** in the core (Topic E.5). This process releases a tremendous amount of energy, which creates outward thermal and radiation pressure. This pressure pushes against the inward pull of gravity.

highlights.text: Nuclear fusion in star's core releases energy creating outward thermal pressure

steps.title: The dynamic feedback mechanism
steps.content:
The star remains stable through a **self-regulating feedback loop**. If the star's core were to contract slightly due to gravity, the temperature and density in the core would increase. 

From our study of **Topic B.3 (Gas Laws)**, we know that higher temperatures lead to faster particle motion. This increases the rate of nuclear fusion, which in turn boosts the outward pressure. This extra pressure causes the star to expand back to its original size, restoring the balance.

steps.title: Establish hydrostatic equilibrium
steps.content: When the inward gravitational force and the outward thermal pressure are exactly equal and opposite, the star is in a state of **hydrostatic equilibrium**. This balance is what ensures the star's stability over the vast majority of its lifetime.

highlights.text: When these forces are in balance (hydrostatic equilibrium), the star remains stable
